content:
Member-only story

Most developers answer this question based on Twitter debates. Here is what ten years of production code actually tells you.

12 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You are about to start learning your first serious programming language, or maybe you are switching stacks after years in another ecosystem. Someone in a Discord server told you TypeScript is the future and you are wasting your time with plain JavaScript. Someone else told you TypeScript is bloated and you should just write good JavaScript. Both of them are arguing based on vibes, not production systems.
I have worked on codebases that were pure JavaScript, codebases that were fully TypeScript, and, perhaps most painfully, codebases that were TypeScript in name only, stuffed with any everywhere because the team bolted it on after the fact and nobody had time to do it properly. I have been in post-mortems where a missing type check contributed to a production outage. I have also been in post-mortems where TypeScript's complexity caused a junior engineer to spend three days fighting a generic constraint instead of fixing the actual bug. Neither camp wins cleanly here. The real answer depends on something most hot takes skip entirely: where you are in your learning journey and what you are trying to build.
